metadata.title:
LIVED HIGH AND
CHARGED It


To STATE

pages.raw_text:
OLYMPIA, Jan. 25-The progress
of the legislative inquiry into the
expense accounts of the state rail-
road commission is bringing that
organization into the limelight as
one of the costliest adjuncts of the
administration.


AS an instance, Commissioner
McMillin lived at the Reliance
club in Seattle at the expense of
the state and it cost the people
about 81 every time McMillin want
ed to eat. When McMillin wanted
to travel he required the drawing
room car. The bill was sent in and
paid by the state.


Fairchild got the habit of charg-
ing everything up to the state also,
as his hotel bills contracted while
in Olympia, where he is supposed to
have headquarters, show. A hotel
bill of $50 for eight days in Wash
ington, d. c., was dug up against
Commissioner Lawrence. The lat
ter explained that he had innocent
ly wandered into high priced
house and it took him more than
week to find it out


The Commission also had a taste
for fine furniture as we'll as nice
things to eat. 1t bought sofa for
$95 and chairs at $50 each. And it
was a generous body to otehr state
departments withal, for it used up
600 worth of stamps, part of
which were stuck on boom litera-
ture the immigration commission
sent out. The state paid the bills.
The railroad commission was also
prolific of a few good jobs. Clyde
Hadley, Supreme Court Judge Had
ley's son, Is drawing salary from
the commission allowance and act
ing as administration publicity
agent, whatever that is, in the gov
ernOr's office. G.. A. Snowden had
some nice trips around the coun-
try as secretary to the commission,
What work he did is not shown.


In defense of their actions Com
missioners Fairchild and Lawrence
were before the committee yester-
day. Lawrence argued that the
commission had saved shippers of
the state $4,000 in rate reduc-
tons. Fairchild argued that all the
expense had been incurred in as
pertaining whether the roads were
Overcharging. This has not yet
been discovered by the commission,
as it will have to learn all the cost
and incidentals of railroad con-
struction before it can arrive at a
conclusion.


The only state railroad commits
sion investigation yet made produc
tive of report was the fuel ia
mine inquiry at Tacoma. That re-
suited in a whitewash for the com
panies, although the fuel situation
is still desperate on the Sound.


AS a result of the discussion OF
the commission extravaganza
Hutchinson. of Spokane, offered 6
resolution calling for a searching
investigation. McMillin was the
special object of his attack. Hutch
inson declared this member hat
been paid $4,000 for which the
state had no return. He also show
ed that McMillin was a favored
shipper while serving as commits
sioner. On motion the resolution
was referred to the railroads com
mittee.
